title: Marine Flora of Oeyondo Islands on the Yellow Sea of Korea

chunks.text:
Algae (The Korean Journal of Phycology) Volume 13(1): 1-11, 1998

Marine Flora of Oeyondo Islands on the Yellow Sea, Korea: I. Green Algae and Seagrasses

Tae Oh Cho and Sung Min Boo*

Department of Biology, Chungnam National University, Daejon 305-764, Korea

A marine floral survey of benthic green algae and seagrasses was carried out in Oeyondo Islands on the Yellow Sea of Korea from 1992 to 1994. A list of nine genera and eighteen species was compiled: 13 green algae and 5 sea- grasses. Detailed illustrations as well as the keys and short descriptions were given for most of the taxa. Key Words: green algae, marine flora, Oeyondo, seagrasses, Yellow Sea

INTRODUCTION

A species list of marine benthic algae from Korea have been published by Lee and Kang (1986). This work has made a compilation of the previous accounts studied from all the coasts and a significant contribution for the further understanding of species diverisity and biogeog- raphy of marine algae of Korea. Many of the marine algal studies have been carried out near the coastal area. The algal floristic accounts for islands isolated far from mainland have rarely been given. The marine algae of the Yellow Sea area have been studied with the reference to floristics and ecology (Okamura 1892, 1914, Cotton 1906, Lee and Lee 1981, Park and Kim 1990, Yoon and Boo 1991). Oeyondo Islands is located on the midst of the Yellow Sea and 50 km far from mainland. The islands are com- posed of several islets and are known for one of rich marine flora in the Yellow Sea. The marine algal list of these islands was compiled by Rho (1958), Kang (1966), Lee and Boo (1988), and Cho and Boo (1996). The biogeo- graphic aspects of the Yellow Sea were discussed by Koh and Oh (1992), and Cho and Boo (1996). The aim of this work is to give detailed illustrations to all the species of marine benthic green algae and sea- grasses for a further guide of marine flora in Oeyondo Islands. Corresponding author (smboo@hanbat.chungnam.ac.kr)

MATERIALS AND METHODS

Three sampling stations were chosen on the littoral of Oeyondo Islands and one in the sublittoral (Fig. 1). Green algae and seagrasses were collected bimonthly from March, 1991 to January, 1993. All the materials were fixed with 3-5% neutral formalin in the field. Observations and drawings were done under Olympus microscope with drawing apparatus. Sections for anatomical examination were made by hands on a slide glass under a stereomicroscope. The size and dimension, otherwise commented, were repre- sented a mean value with standard deviations calculated from measurements of 25 cells, tissues, and plants. Nomeclature of species and the taxonomic arrange- ment mostly accorded with Yoshida et al. (1995). The uncertain species were excluded in the list. All the speci- mens identified in this study have been mounted on herbarium sheets and deposited in the herbarium of Department of Biology, Chungnam National University. LIST OF TAXA

Division Chlorophyta Class Ulvophyceae Order Ulotrichales Family Ulotrichaceae Ulothrix Kiitzing 1833 The genus Ultothrix is lectotypified as U. tenuissimum

Kiitzing and the members are either in marine or fresh- water. The genus is sometimes confused with Urospora by its filamentous habit. Ulothrix flacca (Dillwyn) Thuret, Figs 2 - 3

Plants filamentous, uniseriate, 0.5 cm high. Cells disc to rectangular, 263.8 + 29.4 um 10 long, 437.1 + 28.7 um broad in middle part, elongate near base. Plants epiphyt- ic and epilithic in the littoral in winter and spring, rarely in summer. Note: The marine U. flaccida is known to have diplo- biontic isomorphic life cycles, do as the freshwater species. Order Ulvales Family Monostromataceae Monostroma Thuret 1854 The genus Monostroma is lectotypified as M. oxycoccum (Kutzing) Thuret and is distributed in the northern hemisphere. After isomorphic members were placed in Ulva, Monostroma is sometimes reported to belong to the Ulvaceae. Based on life history, Tatewaki (1972) con- tributed to solve taxonomic accounts for five species from Japan. Key to the species 1. Plants divided in margin and cells arranged compactly ---++s:ssesseeesseeeee rere Monostroma grevillei

1. Plants undulate in margin and cells arranged loosely «s:++1sesseeseeeseeeseeesseeteeeeeeeeeens M. nitidum Monostroma grevillei (Thuret) Wittrock, Figs 4-5

Plants flat, divided in margin, monostromatic, 7.5 cm high, 1.6 + 0.2 cm broad. Cells irregular, one-layered, arranged compactly. Plants epilithic in the littoral in spring. Note: This species is distinguished by having base without true rhizoids, but there are some plants with rhi- zoids in the field. Monostroma nitidum Wittrock, Fig. 6

Plants flat, undulate in margin, 8.5 cm high, and 4.5 + 0.5 cm broad. Cells irregular, one-layered, arranged loosely. Plants epilithic in the littoral in winter and

spring. Family Ulvaceae Key to the genera 1. Plants tubular and separated in margin Lecce ee cece asec aeeeaseneeeeueteaeessesseeeseseaees Enteromorpha 1. Plants membraneous and cell layers distromatic

Enteromorpha Link, 1820

The genus Enteromorpha is lectotypified as E. intesti- nalis (L.) Kiitzing.
The Korean members have been stud- ied by Lee et al. (1986).

Key to the species

1. Plants branched and tubular-:::--+0::e+0seseeeeeeeeee eee 2 1. Plants simple and undulate «--+++++++++-+++++++ E. linza 2. Plants branched in lower part ------+++++- E. intestinalis

2. Plants proliferously branched in all part---E. prolifera Enteromorpha intestinalis (Linnaeus) Link, Figs 7-8

Plants simple, branched near base, tubular, twisted, entire, 24 cm high, 0.4 + 0.1 cm broad. Cells irregularly arranged, two-layered, separated in margin. Plants epilithic in the littoral in summer, fall, and rarely winter. Note: This species occurs frequently in highly polluted area. The plants are very variable, but twisted blade and irregular arrangement of cells are diagnostic features for E. intestinalis. Enteromorpha linza (Linnaeus) J. Agardh, Figs 9-13

Plants simple, leaf-shaped, compressed, undulated in margin, entire, 9.5 cm high, 2.0 + 0.6 cm broad. Cells reg- ularly arranged, two-layered, separated in margin. Rhizoids unicelled. Zoosporangia formed in the upper, with three to seven spores. Plants epilithic in the littoral in winter, spring, and rarely in summer and reproduc- tive in spring. Note: This species is easily distinguished by having

very simple thallus. Enteromorpha prolifera (Miiller) J. Agardh

Plants with axes, proliferous, branched, 11 cm high, 0.2 + 0.1 cm broad. Branches numerous, 124.1 + 12.5 um broad. Cells longitudinally or sometimes transversally arranged, distromatic. Plants epilithic in the littoral in fall and winter. Note: This species is so variable that several varieties have been recognized in New Zealand (Womersley 1984). Ulva Linnaeus, 1753

The genus Ulva is typified as U. lactuca Linnaeus. The members are widely distributed in the world and grow on the various substrate. The gametes and zoospores of some members are reported to be released at intervals of two weeks during the spring tide. The Korean members have been studied by Lee et al.

(1986). Ulva pertusa Kjellmann, Figs 14-17

Plants membraneous, round, undulate in margin, irregularly lobed, perforated, 15 cm broad. Cells round to polygonal, 33.7 + 2.8 x 21.8 + 2.6 yum in the lower layer, 22.9 + 1.9 x 17.9 + 2.7 um in the upper layer. Zoosporangia formed in the upper thallus. Plants epilithic in the littoral, reproductive in september. Note: U. pertusa occurs in the North eastern Pacific. Order Cladophorales

Family Cladophoraceae Cladophora Kitzing 1843

The genus Cladophora is typified as C. oligoclona (Ktitzing) Kiitzing and the members are widely distrib- uted in the world. van den Hoek (1984) has intensively studied this genus based on morphology, biogeography and life history. Oh (1990) has studied the Korean mem- bers. Key to the species 1. Plants slender with simple branchlets

Li eeeeecaaeeeeeeeeeeeeeneeeseeeaaeeseeneaees Cladophora gracilis 1. Plants dense with secund branchlets -------:- C. opaca

Cladophora gracilis Ktitzing, Figs 18-19 Plants slender, glauceous green, 11 cm high. Branches di- to trichotomous and secund.

Upper branch- es straight and simple. Cells 315.8 + 77.9 um long, 92.9 + 6.9 um broad. Apical cells acute, 126 + 22.3 um long, 30.0 + 3.5 um broad. Plants epilithic in the sublittoral in sum- mer. Cladophora opaca Sakai, Figs 20-21

Plants dense, yellowish green, 11 cm high. Branches dichotomous to trichotomous, alternative. Upper

Cho & Boo: Marine Green Flora of Oeyondo Islands 5

branches straight to curved, with secund branchlets. Cells 426.8 + 68.0 um long, 66.0 + 7.4 ym broad. Apical cells acute, 132.8 + 18.9 um long, 32.34 + 1.9 um_ broad. Plants epilithic in the littoral in summer. Order Codiales Family Bryopsidaceae Bryopsis Lamouroux, 1809 The genus Bryopsis is typified as B. pennata Lamouroux.
They are distributed on the temperate to the tropical coasts.

Key to the species 1. Plant pinnate form with simple filaments bee e te eeeeaeeeeeeeeesueeeeeeaeeaauneceeane enna Bryopsis maxima 1. Plant pinnate form with bilateral branchlets Lecce a eeee ea eeeeaeeeeeaeeeeaaeeeaaeeeeeneeeeees see B, plumosa Bryopsis maxima Okamura Plants etect, arising from branched rhizoids, 3 + 0.3 cm high. Branches pinnate, with simple branchlets. Plants epiphytic or epilithic in the littroal fringe. Note: Descriptions and illustrations of Korean plants

.are firstly given here, but our plants are very small com-

pared to the Japanese ones with 40 cm in length. Bryopsis plumosa (Hudson) C. Agardh

Plants erect, arising from rhizoids, 2 + 0.1 cm high. Branches bilateral, with bilateral branchlets. Plants epi- phytic or epilithic in the littroal fringe. Note: B. plumosa is reported to be common in the tem- perate region from the Southern and the Nothern Hemisphere. Family Codiaceae Codium Stackhouse, 1797

The genus Codium is typified as C. tomentosum (Hudson) Stackhouse and includes about 80 species in the world. Habit and utricle are diagnostic features for the species. The Korean members were studied by Oh et al. (1987).

Key to the species

1. Plants crustose with obtuse utricles

Lecce a eeeeaueceeaeeeeeaaresuaeeeeaneeceneees Codium adhaerens 1. Plants erect, dichotomous with acute utricles

Codium adhaerens (Cabrera) C. Agardh, Figs 22-23 Plants crustose, 8 cm broad, 0.6 + 0.1 cm thick. Utricles obtuse, 927.9 + 120.5 ym long, 143.8 + 18.1 um broad. New urticles formed in lower part of old utricles. Female gametangia produced laterally from utricles, 319.2 + 40.2 pm long, 129.7 + 29.0 um broad. Plants epilithic in the

59. Form of serrated seed. sublittoral and reproductive in late summer. Note: This species looks like C. convolutum (Dellow) Silva, C. dimorphism Svedelius, and C. setchellii Gardner in being prostrate and cushion-like. Codium fragile (Suringar) Hariot, Figs 24-27

Plants terete, tubular, dichotomous, 10 cm high, 0.6 + 0.1 cm broad. Utricles acute, 977.9 + 80.8 um long, 312.9 + 18.2 ym broad. Hairs produced on utricles, 455.5 +

151.3 um long, and 49.9 + 5.8 ym broad. Male gametan- gia produced laterally from utricles, 231.9 + 19.5 ym long, 80.3 + 8.5 um broad. Plants epilithic in the littoral in summer, fall and winter, reproductive in late summer.

Note: C. fragile is similar to C. dichotomum (Hudson) S.F. Gray in dichotomous branching and shape of thalli, but the utricle is obtuse in the latter. 10 Algae Vol. 13(1), 1998

Division Magnoliophyta

Class Liliopsida

Order Potamogetonales Family Zosteraceae Key to the genera 1. Leaves dendate and rhizomes with short internodes benecceaaeceeceeeenaaeeceeaneeeeeaseeneaseenneeeeaaes Phyllospadix 1. Leaves entire and rhizomes with long internodes Lecececeeeeeetseceaenecseeseesaeceseeeeseeenee seen ee eentees Zostera

Phyllospadix Hooker, 1838

Phillips and Menez (1988) reviewed the Phyllospadix members in the world. They often grow on the surfy and rocky shore. The following two members have been studied by Shin et al. (1993).

Key to the species

1. Leaves di- to tridentate and veins five ---P. iwatensis

1. Leaves dentate and veins three -+---++++++- P. japonica Phyllospadix iwatensis Makino, Figs 28-38

Plants 46 cm high. Leaves obtuse in tip, five in veins in the upper part, 0.18 + 0.03 cm broad, di- and tridentate with spine cells in margin, with one or two sponge lay- ers in cross section view of middle part. Seeds liver- shaped, with two lobes. Plants epilithic in the tide pool throughout year and reproductive in spring. Note: This species often occurs in exposed area. Phyllospadix japonica Makino

Plants 40 cm high. Leaves obtuse in tip, three in vein, 0.15 cm broad, di- and tridentate with spine cells in mar- gin, with one or two sponge layers in cross section view. Seeds liver-shaped, with two lobes. Plants epilithic in the exposed littoral and reproductive in spring. Note: This species often occurs in the sheltered area. Zostera Linnaeus, 1753

The genus Zostera is typified as Z. marina Linnaeus and includes 12 species in the world. Phillips and Menez (1988) reviewed the Zostera members.

in the world. The members often grow in the muddy and shel-

tered area. Key to the species 1. Plants small, leaves narrow and veins three bocce eee secu eee ceaeceu secu eeuseaeesaecepecaeeneties Z. japonica 1. Plants tall, leaves wide, veins more than five -:---: 2

2. Leave tips mucronate and seeds with rough testa bocce eec cae eeeaeeceasecaeeeeeeeeaaeeeeeseanceeeseeeeeas Z. marina 2. Leave tips obtuse and seeds with smooth testa ESSE TST SSeSSSTESSTSSSSTTeSS Z..caulescens Zostera japonica Ascherson and Graebner, Figs.39-41 Plants 34 cm high, submerged. Leaves obtuse, three in vein, 0.2 cm broad. Seeds 2.5-0.7 cm long, 0.2 cm broad. Seeds with rough testa. Plants on muddy area in the sub- littoral zone and reproductive in summer. Note: This species resembles Z. nana Roth in having narrow leaves with three veins, that needs a further study on the Korean plants. Zostera caulescens Miki, Figs 42-52

Plants 165 cm‘high, submerged. Leaves obtuse, seven in vein, 0.8 + 0.1 cm. Stems cylindrical, 0.5 + 0.1 cm broad, with internodes of 22.0 + 8.6 cm. Seeds with smooth testa, 9.9 + 1.3 cm long, 0.9 + 0.1 cm broad. Plants on muddy area in the sublittoral and reproductive in summer. Note: The Korean plants are variable in the number of vein. We found plants with 5 or 7 veins. This needs a fur- ther study how the number of veins can differ in a single location. Zostera marina Linnaeus, Figs 53-59

Plants 27 cm tall, submerged. Leaves mucronate, seven in vein, 0.5-0.1 cm broad. Stem 0.3 + 0.1 cm broad, with internodes of 7.6 + 2.2 cm. Seeds 5.8-1.2 cm long, 0.5 + 0.1 cm broad, with rough testa. Plants on muddy area in the sublittoral and reproductive in summer. Note: The plants with seven veins are easily confused with Z. caulacanthus, but differ from the latter in having rough testa of seeds.
